title: The Morning Sunlight Protocol: Why This Free Biohack Is Transforming My Clients' Energy, Hormones, and Sleep
excerpt: Forget expensive gadgets—the most powerful biohack for women's hormones, energy, and longevity costs nothing and takes just 10 minutes each morning.
body:
If you caught my recent Instagram reel about morning sunlight and cortisol, you probably noticed I was practically giddy about this topic. And for good reason—after years of testing supplements, gadgets, and protocols that promise to optimize everything from my mitochondria to my mood, I keep coming back to one intervention that outperforms almost all of them.

It's free. It requires no equipment. And it takes less time than brewing your morning coffee.

I'm talking about deliberate morning sunlight exposure—what I call the Morning Sunlight Protocol. And today, I want to give you the deep dive this powerful biohack deserves, because understanding why it works will help you commit to making it a non-negotiable part of your routine.

What Gary Brecka Gets Right About Free Biohacks

You may have seen the recent buzz around Gary Brecka's list of six free biohacks, where he called morning sunlight exposure "one of the most potent metabolic tools available." He's absolutely right—and this aligns perfectly with what the latest evidence-based biohacking research is confirming.

In a world obsessed with expensive NAD supplements and longevity travel retreats (yes, that's a thing now), it's almost radical to suggest that the sun rising outside your window might be your most powerful tool. But the science doesn't lie.

The Science: What Happens When Light Hits Your Eyes

Let's get nerdy for a moment, because understanding the mechanism makes this practice stick.

When morning sunlight enters your eyes—specifically, when it hits specialized cells called intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs)—it sends a direct signal to your suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), the master clock in your brain. This signal essentially tells your body: "It's morning. Time to wake up and perform."

This triggers a cascade of hormonal events:

  • Cortisol rises appropriately. Yes, cortisol gets a bad reputation, but a healthy morning cortisol spike (called the Cortisol Awakening Response or CAR) is essential for energy, focus, and metabolism. Problems arise when this spike is blunted or delayed—which happens when we wake up in dim rooms scrolling our phones.
  • A timer starts for melatonin. Here's where it gets fascinating: morning light exposure sets a 12-14 hour timer for melatonin release. Get sunlight at 7 AM, and your body is primed to produce melatonin around 9-10 PM. This is why morning light directly improves your sleep that night.
  • Dopamine and serotonin production increases. Sunlight stimulates the production of these crucial neurotransmitters, affecting mood, motivation, and even food cravings throughout the day.

A 2023 study published in the Journal of Pineal Research found that participants who got 30+ minutes of morning light exposure had 58% better sleep efficiency and significantly lower rates of depressive symptoms compared to those who avoided morning light.

Why This Matters More for Women

Here's something that often gets overlooked in mainstream biohacking content (which, let's be honest, is usually designed with men in mind): women's circadian rhythms are more sensitive and more complex than men's.

Our hormonal cycles—estrogen, progesterone, cortisol, thyroid hormones—are intricately tied to our circadian rhythm. When our master clock is dysregulated, it doesn't just affect sleep. It affects:

  • Menstrual cycle regularity. Research shows that women with disrupted circadian rhythms have higher rates of irregular periods and more severe PMS symptoms.
  • Thyroid function. TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) follows a circadian pattern. Chronic circadian disruption can contribute to subclinical hypothyroid symptoms—fatigue, weight gain, brain fog, cold intolerance.
  • Perimenopause and menopause symptoms. Women in perimenopause often experience a degradation of circadian rhythm strength, contributing to sleep issues, hot flashes, and mood changes. Morning light is one of the most effective ways to strengthen that rhythm.
  • Fertility. Multiple studies have linked shift work and circadian disruption to reduced fertility. While morning sunlight isn't a fertility treatment, supporting your natural rhythms creates an optimal hormonal environment.

This is why I'm so passionate about this topic. It's not just a productivity hack—it's foundational women's health.

The Morning Sunlight Protocol: How to Do It Right

Based on the research and my own experimentation, here's the protocol I recommend:

Timing

Get outside within 30-60 minutes of waking, ideally within the first hour after sunrise. The light at this time has a specific spectrum (more blue light, less UV) that's optimal for circadian entrainment without skin damage concerns.

Duration

On a clear, sunny day: 10-15 minutes is sufficient.

On a cloudy or overcast day: Aim for 20-30 minutes. Yes, even on cloudy days, outdoor light is significantly brighter than indoor light—often 10-50 times brighter.

During winter or at higher latitudes: You may need 30-45 minutes, and a 10,000 lux light therapy box can supplement (but not replace) outdoor exposure on very dark mornings.

The Rules

  • No sunglasses. The light needs to enter your eyes. Regular prescription glasses are fine.
  • No looking directly at the sun. This should be obvious, but I'll say it anyway. You're looking toward the bright sky, not at the sun itself.
  • Outside, not through a window. Standard window glass blocks many of the wavelengths your eyes need for circadian entrainment. Step outside, onto a balcony, or open the window.
  • Consistency matters more than perfection. Five minutes every day is better than 30 minutes twice a week.

How I Stack This Protocol

Because I'm a biohacker at heart, I love stacking multiple interventions. Here's my typical morning routine that builds on this foundation:

6:30 AM: Wake naturally (no alarm when possible—that's a topic for another post).

6:35 AM: Step outside with my first glass of water (with a pinch of sea salt and a squeeze of lemon). The combination of hydration, minerals, and light exposure is synergistic.

6:35-6:50 AM: I do gentle movement while I'm out there—sometimes yoga, sometimes just walking around my backyard barefoot (grounding, another free biohack). If it's cold, I embrace it briefly—yes, there's overlap with cold exposure benefits here.

6:50 AM: Come inside and delay coffee for at least 90 minutes after waking (this supports healthy cortisol patterns—see Andrew Huberman's work on this).

This entire sequence costs nothing and sets the trajectory for my entire day.

What Changes When You Commit to This Practice

In my experience—both personally and with the women I work with—here's what typically shifts within 2-4 weeks of consistent morning light exposure:

Week 1: You may feel more alert in the mornings. Afternoon slumps become less severe. Some people notice they're slightly sleepier earlier in the evening.

Week 2: Sleep onset becomes easier. You stop lying awake for 30+ minutes. Dream recall often increases (a sign of healthier sleep architecture).

Week 3-4: Energy levels stabilize. That 3 PM desperate need for caffeine or sugar diminishes. Mood becomes more consistent. If you track HRV, you'll likely see improvements.

Long-term: Hormonal symptoms often improve—less severe PMS, more regular cycles, better stress resilience. This is the cumulative effect of a well-regulated circadian system supporting your entire endocrine cascade.

Troubleshooting Common Obstacles

"I wake up before sunrise." This is common, especially in winter. Do your light exposure as soon as the sun is up. In the meantime, use a 10,000 lux light therapy box positioned at eye level during your morning routine. It's not identical to sunlight, but it helps.

"I work from home and never go outside." This is actually one of the biggest modern health challenges. Make morning light non-negotiable—even if it means standing on your porch in your pajamas for 10 minutes. Stack it with something you already do: take your coffee outside, do your morning reading outdoors, take a call while walking.

"It's too cold/hot/rainy." The light still works through clouds. And brief exposure to uncomfortable temperatures can actually enhance the benefits (hello, hormesis). Dress appropriately and start small.

"I forget." Tie it to an existing habit. Coffee? Take it outside. Morning bathroom visit? Walk outside immediately after. Phone scrolling? Do it outside.

The Bigger Picture: Why Free Biohacks Deserve More Attention

I want to end with a philosophical point that I think matters.

We're in an era of longevity travel, $500 supplement stacks, and gadgets that promise to optimize every biomarker. And while I love data and I'll never stop experimenting, I think we sometimes overlook the profound power of free, ancestral-aligned interventions.

Morning sunlight isn't just a biohack. It's what human biology expects. It's what our bodies evolved with over millions of years. When we provide it, we're not adding something extra—we're correcting a deficit that modern life created.

The same is true for many of the most powerful interventions: sleep, movement, real food, social connection, time in nature, stress management.

So yes, I'll keep exploring the cutting edge of longevity science. But I'll never stop advocating for the basics—because they work, they're accessible, and they remind us that our bodies are fundamentally wise.

Your Practical Takeaways

  1. Start tomorrow. Set an intention tonight to get outside within an hour of waking. Even five minutes counts.
  2. Track it simply. Use a habit tracker or just put a checkmark on your calendar. Consistency is everything.
  3. Stack it. Combine morning light with hydration, movement, or grounding to amplify benefits.
  4. Be patient. Give it 2-4 weeks before evaluating. Circadian shifts take time.
  5. Extend the practice. Throughout the day, seek natural light. Eat lunch outside. Take walking meetings. Reduce artificial light at night.

This is biohacking at its most elegant: simple, free, and profoundly effective. I can't wait to hear how it works for you.

Drop a comment or DM me on Instagram—I read everything. ☀️

title: The 5 Longevity Supplements Actually Worth Taking in 2025
excerpt: NMN, Spermidine, Berberine — the supplement space is noisy. Here's what the research actually says and what I personally take each morning.
body:
The longevity supplement space has exploded over the last five years. Walk into any health store and you'll find shelves overflowing with promises — and a lot of very expensive urine.

I've spent the last two years tracking the research, running personal experiments, and consulting with functional medicine practitioners to cut through the noise. Here are the five supplements that consistently show up in both the peer-reviewed literature and my own health data.

1. NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide)

NMN is a precursor to NAD+, a molecule that declines dramatically as we age and is essential for cellular energy production. David Sinclair's research at Harvard has been instrumental in bringing this to mainstream attention.

What the research says: Human trials show NMN supplementation raises blood NAD+ levels, improves muscle insulin sensitivity, and may improve sleep quality. The studies are still relatively short-term, but the mechanism is solid.

My protocol: 500mg in the morning with a small amount of fat for absorption. I use a sublingual form for better bioavailability.

2. Spermidine

Found naturally in wheat germ, aged cheese, and mushrooms, spermidine triggers autophagy — your body's cellular cleanup process. Think of it as a deep clean for your cells.

What the research says: A 2021 randomized controlled trial showed spermidine supplementation improved memory in older adults. Animal studies show dramatic lifespan extension. Human longevity data is still emerging.

My protocol: 1mg daily from a wheat germ extract supplement.

3. Berberine

Often called "nature's Ozempic," berberine is a plant compound that activates AMPK — essentially the same pathway that makes caloric restriction and exercise beneficial for longevity.

What the research says: Multiple meta-analyses show berberine effectively lowers blood glucose, improves lipid panels, and reduces inflammation markers. Some studies show comparable effects to metformin for blood sugar management.

My protocol: 500mg with lunch and dinner. Cycling 2 months on, 2 weeks off.

4. Magnesium Glycinate

This one isn't glamorous but it might be the highest-impact supplement on this list. Studies suggest up to 68% of adults are deficient in magnesium, which is involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions.

What the research says: Magnesium supplementation is consistently associated with better sleep quality, reduced cortisol, improved insulin sensitivity, and lower cardiovascular risk.

My protocol: 400mg magnesium glycinate 30 minutes before bed. This form is best absorbed and doesn't cause digestive upset.

5. Omega-3 (High-Dose EPA/DHA)

Not sexy, but the evidence base here is unmatched. The VITAL study — 25,000 participants over five years — showed significant reductions in cardiovascular events and cancer mortality with omega-3 supplementation.

What the research says: Reduces systemic inflammation (lower hs-CRP), supports brain health, improves triglycerides, and may slow cellular aging via telomere preservation.

My protocol: 2–3g combined EPA/DHA daily from a high-quality triglyceride-form fish oil. I test my Omega-3 Index annually — aiming for 8%+.

The Bottom Line

No supplement replaces sleep, movement, whole foods, and stress management. But these five have enough evidence behind them that I consider them worth the investment — especially if you're already doing the fundamentals well.

As always, talk to your doctor before starting any new supplement protocol, especially if you're on medications.

title: Why Your Sleep Tracker Is Lying to You
excerpt: The truth about HRV, deep sleep metrics, and what actually matters when optimizing your recovery.
body:
You wake up after eight hours feeling groggy. Your Oura ring says you got 1h 20min of deep sleep and your HRV is 62. Is that good? Should you push through your workout or rest? What does any of this actually mean?

Sleep trackers have democratized access to sleep data that was previously only available in sleep labs. But there's a problem: most people are using this data in ways that are actively making their sleep worse.

The Accuracy Problem

Consumer wearables measure sleep stages primarily through actigraphy (movement) and heart rate patterns. The gold standard — polysomnography in a sleep lab — uses EEG to directly measure brain waves.

A 2019 study comparing consumer wearables to PSG found that devices correctly identified sleep stages only 69% of the time. Deep sleep (slow-wave sleep) had the lowest accuracy. Your tracker's confident-sounding "1h 22min of deep sleep" is genuinely an educated guess.

What HRV Actually Tells You

Heart rate variability — the variation in time between heartbeats — is one of the more reliable metrics consumer devices measure. But it's widely misunderstood.

HRV doesn't tell you if you're healthy. It tells you about your autonomic nervous system's current state. Your HRV is individual to you — comparing your 58ms to someone else's 95ms is meaningless. What matters is your trend over time and deviations from your personal baseline.

The Orthosomnia Trap

Researchers have identified a phenomenon called orthosomnia: anxiety about achieving perfect sleep metrics, which ironically worsens sleep. The tracker creates the problem it's supposed to solve.

If you check your sleep score the moment you wake up, and that number influences how you feel and perform that day, you may be in this trap.

What I Actually Pay Attention To

After two years of tracking obsessively and then stepping back, here's what I've landed on as actually useful signals:

HRV trend (not daily number): A downward trend over 5–7 days signals accumulated stress or illness before I consciously feel it. Useful for catching overtraining or pre-illness.

Resting heart rate: More accurately measured than sleep stages. Elevated RHR (3–5bpm above baseline) is a reliable signal to reduce training intensity.

Time in bed vs. time asleep: If there's a big gap, it points to a sleep onset or maintenance problem worth addressing.

Subjective feel: How do you actually feel 30 minutes after waking? This correlates with cognitive performance better than any wearable metric.

The Bottom Line

Trackers are tools, not oracles. Use them to spot trends over weeks and months, not to judge individual nights. And if checking your sleep score is the first thing you do every morning — consider a tracking break. Your sleep might actually improve.

title: My 30-Day Cold Plunge Experiment: What Actually Changed
excerpt: I committed to cold exposure every morning for a month. Here's what the data showed — and what I felt.
body:
Cold plunging has gone from fringe biohacking to wellness mainstream in the last three years. Andrew Huberman, Wim Hof, Peter Attia — virtually every longevity-focused voice is talking about deliberate cold exposure. I decided to stop reading about it and actually do it.

For 30 consecutive days, I did 3 minutes of cold exposure every morning. Some days a cold shower, some days a cold plunge tub I borrowed from a friend. Water temperature ranged from 48–58°F (9–14°C).

The Protocol

I kept everything else as controlled as possible — same sleep schedule, diet, training load. I tracked HRV daily (Oura), mood subjectively (1–10 scale), and energy levels at 10am and 3pm. I also did a simple reaction time test (a free app) as a rough cognitive proxy.

Week 1: It Was Miserable

I won't romanticize this. The first week was genuinely unpleasant. The anticipatory anxiety before each session was almost worse than the cold itself. I found myself bargaining — maybe just 90 seconds today, maybe I'll do it tonight instead.

What kept me going was focusing on the exhale. Andrew Huberman's advice to extend the exhale to calm the nervous system actually works. By day 5, I could get in and regulated within about 20 seconds.

Week 2–3: The Shift

Something changed around day 10. I started waking up before my alarm — not dramatically, but consistently 10–15 minutes before it. My 10am energy ratings went from averaging 6.2/10 in week 1 to 7.8/10 in week 3.

My HRV trended upward — not dramatically, but my 7-day average moved from 61ms to 68ms. Given that I changed nothing else, the cold exposure is the most plausible variable.

Week 4: New Normal

By the final week, I was looking forward to it. Not in a "this feels amazing" way — more like how I feel about a hard workout. I wanted the feeling after.

Mood scores averaged 7.4/10 in week 4 vs. 6.5/10 in week 1. Reaction time improved by approximately 8% (from ~285ms average to ~262ms). These are small-sample, self-reported numbers — not science. But they reflect what I experienced.

What I Think Is Actually Happening

Cold exposure triggers norepinephrine release — studies show increases of 200–300% after cold immersion. Norepinephrine drives focus, mood, and energy. It also has anti-inflammatory effects.

The mental training component may be as valuable as the physiological. Getting into cold water every morning when you don't want to builds a tolerance for discomfort that seems to transfer to other areas — harder workouts, difficult conversations, staying present when things get uncomfortable.

Would I Recommend It?

Yes, with caveats. Start with cold showers — 30 seconds at the end of a warm shower — before investing in a plunge tub. Build the protocol slowly. And if you have cardiovascular conditions, talk to your doctor first; cold exposure causes acute increases in heart rate and blood pressure.

After 30 days, I've continued. Not every day — maybe 4–5 times per week. The benefits feel real enough to maintain.
